title: Glamorgan Sausages
subtitle: Welsh cheese, leek, and herb sausages, fried until golden
excerpt: Glamorgan sausages are a Welsh vegetarian sausage made from Caerphilly cheese, leek, and breadcrumbs, shaped into cylinders and fried until golden. They have no meat and no binding other than egg; the texture is creamy inside and crisp outside. They are substantial enough to serve as a main dish with chutney, or alongside a roast as an alternative to a meat sausage.
sourceNotes: Glamorgan sausages are a traditional Welsh dish, named for the county of Glamorgan in South Wales. George Borrow, Wild Wales (1862): Glamorgan sausages mentioned as a regional speciality. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): Glamorgan sausage recipe. Traditional Welsh vegetarian dish.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before frying on a tray, then bag. Fry from frozen over a lower heat for slightly longer.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape the sausages and refrigerate for up to 24 hours before frying.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mild, crumbly white Welsh cheese with a clean, slightly salty, lactic flavour. It melts into a creamy mass when cooked rather than stringing like Cheddar. If unavailable, a mild crumbly Lancashire or a young Cheddar can substitute, though the flavour is slightly different.

content.text: The texture of a good Glamorgan sausage is creamy and savoury inside with a light, golden crust.

is the traditional cheese and gives the most authentic result; it crumbles easily into the breadcrumb mixture and has a clean flavour that does not overpower the leek. Chilling the shaped sausages for 30 minutes before frying firms them enough to hold their shape in the pan.

items.prepNote: white and pale green part only, very finely chopped
from day-old white bread, plus extra for coating
1 beaten into the mix, 1 beaten for coating

content.text: Combine the {{caerphilly-cheese}}, {{leek}}, {{breadcrumbs-fresh}}, thyme, {{dijon-mustard}}, {{sea-salt-flakes}}, and {{black-pepper}} in a bowl. Add 1 beaten {{eggs}} and mix until the mixture holds together when squeezed. Divide into 8 equal portions and shape each into a sausage about 8 cm long. Dip in beaten egg, then roll in extra breadcrumbs. Place on a plate and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large frying pan over a medium heat. Fry the sausages for 3 to 4 minutes per side, turning carefully, until golden-brown on all surfaces and hot through. Drain briefly on kitchen paper. Serve immediately.

rows.symptom: Sausages falling apart in the pan
rows.cause: Mix too dry, not enough egg, or not chilled before frying
rows.fix: Chill the shaped sausages for at least 30 minutes before frying. If the mix was dry, add a little more beaten egg when mixing.

rows.symptom: Exterior burning before the centre heats through
rows.fix: Fry over a medium rather than high heat. The sausages are thick and need time for the heat to reach the centre.

content.text: Glamorgan sausages are a Welsh regional speciality that became known across Britain through Jane Grigson's writing in the 1970s. They are one of the older documented British vegetarian dishes, eaten in Wales long before vegetarianism became a dietary movement. They are excellent with a chutney, particularly a tomato or onion one, and work well as a starter or a main dish served with good bread and a salad.
